title: Living Docs Learning Ledger
subtitle: What the living-doc practice is teaching us across monitors, workstreams, issue runs, dossiers, and skills.
scope: Method-level reflection over living docs maintained from the local catalog and the living-doc-compositor repo.
sourceCoverage: Grounded in the local GTD living-doc catalog, the compositor-owned living docs in docs/, the convergence-type registry, and the repo history inspected on 2026-05-03.
objective: Make the lessons from repeated living-doc use visible as a maintained surface, so future work can see which document shapes are carrying value, which patterns are still weak, and which practices should become templates, skills, or governance checks.
successCondition: A contributor can open this ledger and understand what the living-doc system has learned from actual use, which reusable patterns are trusted, which awkward attempts should not be repeated blindly, and what checks are needed before the method is treated as mature.

objectiveFacets.name: Method learning
objectiveFacets.description: The ledger captures lessons about living docs as a practice, not only status about one project.

objectiveFacets.name: Registry language
objectiveFacets.description: The ledger watches which convergence types keep proving useful and which type boundaries need sharpening.

objectiveFacets.name: Work-surface value
objectiveFacets.description: The ledger preserves what living docs make easier for humans and agents during active work.

objectiveFacets.name: Evidence discipline
objectiveFacets.description: The ledger keeps source coverage, attempts, and verification distinct from interpretation.

objectiveFacets.name: Next method moves
objectiveFacets.description: The ledger turns reflection into concrete follow-up work.

objectiveFacets.name: Parser proof depth
objectiveFacets.description: The ledger preserves what the TrackAndBack parser work taught about proof, autonomy boundaries, replay, blockers, and production hardening.

invariants.name: Reflection stays grounded
invariants.statement: Method claims in this ledger must point back to living docs, registry types, repo history, or actual work patterns rather than becoming abstract doctrine.

invariants.name: Registry before special case
invariants.statement: When a repeated practice appears, first test whether the current convergence-type registry already expresses it before adding new structure.

invariants.name: Attempts stay visible
invariants.statement: Awkward, partial, or superseded patterns remain visible because they explain the current method and prevent repeated rediscovery.

invariants.name: Reflection becomes checks
invariants.statement: Every reflective claim should eventually become a verification checkpoint, template change, skill update, or explicit decision to leave the method alone.

invariants.name: Proof preserves autonomy boundary
invariants.statement: When a proof claims autonomous parser evolution, manual steering, manual graph edits, and operator-selected rescue paths must be separated from qualifying proof evidence.

callouts.title: How to read this ledger
items: Status Snapshot compresses what we inspected at creation time.
Objective Coherence is derived from the root facets, coverage edges, and invariants.
Method Decisions records lessons that already look stable.
Current Capability Surface names what the living-doc system can now do because of repeated use.
Attempts records the shape changes that got us here.
Verification Checkpoints turns reflection into follow-up work.

sections.title: Status Snapshot
sections.rationale: The ledger needs a compact opening view of the inspected living-doc estate before it makes method claims.

callout.title: Initial read
items: The living-doc practice now spans engineering workstreams, issue investigations, monitoring trackers, product seams, reading-system essays, and AI-assisted export work.

The TrackAndBack parser work is the deepest stress test so far: it forced the method to handle proof ladders, deterministic production hardening, replay evidence, formal blockers, and explicit autonomy boundaries.
The repeated section shapes show the work keeps needing decisions, status, indicators, capabilities, proofs, sources, tooling, code anchors, and attempts.
The next gap is cross-doc learning: not more individual status, but a maintained surface for what the method itself has learned.

data.name: Initial registry and corpus counts
notes: Counted from scripts/living-doc-registry.json and docs/*.json on 2026-05-03.

The top recurring convergence types were decision-record, status-snapshot, indicator-trace, capability-surface, proof-ladder, citation-feed, tooling-surface, investigation-findings, competitor-stance-track, and position-cluster-map.

sections.title: Objective Coherence
sections.rationale: This derived view keeps the reflection tied to explicit facets, coverage edges, and invariants instead of becoming free-form commentary.

sections.title: Method Decisions
sections.rationale: The ledger needs a compact ground-truth surface for lessons that have repeated across enough docs to treat as current operating principles.

data.name: The objective is the anchor
notes: The strongest living docs state what the work is trying to make true before listing entities, tickets, sources, or outputs.
When the objective is weak, sections become inventories; when it is clear, sections become purposeful projections.

data.name: The registry is becoming the shared language
notes: The value is not only in rendered pages; it is in reusable convergence types that let humans and agents recognize the same work shape.
New domains should first ask whether an existing type expresses the needed distinction.

data.name: Attempts are durable assets
notes: Issue runs and AI-pass workstreams showed that what was tried and what it proved often matters as much as the final state.
Preserving rejected and superseded attempts prevents future workers from re-learning the same constraint.

data.name: Monitoring docs need cadence, not finality
notes: AI labs, inference economics, AI labor, datacenter, and on-device AI docs are period surfaces.
Their discipline comes from dated sources, indicator movement, stance changes, and prediction resolution.

data.name: Rendered docs must carry the complete tool
notes: Standalone HTML is useful because the reader receives the full compositor, not a reduced report.
This keeps review, exploration, and local library use available after sharing or rendering.

data.name: The method needs its own maintained surface
notes: The body of work has grown large enough that lessons cannot live only in chat or scattered prose.
This ledger exists to keep cross-doc learning inspectable and revisitable.

data.name: TrackAndBack parser work is the deepest method stress test
notes: The parser work forced living docs to carry more than implementation status: proof level, operating model, production intake, formal blockers, replayability, evidence sanitation, and the boundary between autonomous proof and operator rescue.
The relevant surfaces are the parser pipeline overview, the proof ladder overview, the production hardening flywheel, and the bootstrap proof harness.
This is the strongest evidence that living docs are useful when the work is both technical and epistemic: the central question is not only what changed, but what was actually proven.

data.name: Headless proof drift needs a direct-log short loop
notes: The current frontier is not only documenting invariants; it is containing drift across an interactive session, a standalone worker, and nested headless `codex exec` inference.

The wrapper process is not the full proof surface when it launches a nested proof/evolution worker. The direct JSONL event stream and matching runtime log are part of the evidence that decides whether the run completed, blocked, crashed early, or silently drifted.

The containment pattern is: run the deterministic standalone command, tail the nested inference logs, inspect terminal result and premature errors, create a GitHub blocker issue for any failure, stop for owner approval, implement only the approved issue, and rerun the standalone process.

sections.title: Current Capability Surface
sections.rationale: The learning ledger should name the capabilities that emerged from repeated living-doc use, not only the principles behind them.

data.name: Registry-driven composition
notes: The compositor renders many domains through registry-defined convergence types instead of hard-coded domain knowledge.

data.name: Standalone rendering
notes: A source JSON can become a self-contained HTML artifact with the embedded compositor.

data.name: Local library discovery
notes: The private catalog can be exported into a local manifest, which gives the compositor a browsable local library without exposing private paths in shared mode.

data.name: Monitoring surfaces
notes: Competitor, labor, infrastructure, protocol, and inference-economics docs now share a pattern of stance, moves, indicators, sources, position maps, and period notes.

data.name: Issue and implementation run surfaces
notes: Issue run docs preserve orbit, findings, code anchors, verification, and attempts so implementation context survives beyond a single session.

data.name: Cross-doc reflection
notes: This ledger initializes the surface, but recurring sync and promotion rules still need to be tested over time.

data.name: Parser proof and production hardening surfaces
notes: TrackAndBack now has linked living-doc surfaces for pipeline state, staged proof, production parser hardening, and bootstrap proof isolation.
The method carries the proof ladder separately from production readiness gates, issue orbit, tooling, intake shape, and operating decisions.

sections.title: Method Attempts
sections.rationale: The ledger should keep visible the major practice shifts that shaped the current method, including partial and superseded forms.

data.name: Moved from status page toward active work surface
what_tried: Use living docs not only to summarize a completed state, but to coordinate active implementation, investigation, monitoring, and editorial work.
what_proved: The format becomes more valuable when it preserves objective, current state, decisions, code anchors, attempts, and verification in one navigable artifact.
data.cost_to_apply: Requires sharper section typing and regular updates.

data.name: Treated monitors as period surfaces
what_tried: Use stance tracks, move logs, indicator traces, position maps, sources, and period notes for AI market and labor topics.
what_proved: The pattern can carry volatile domains if every update is dated and source-backed.
data.cost_to_apply: Needs an explicit sync cadence and source discipline.

data.name: Derived readable dossiers from monitor docs
what_tried: Publish readable dossier pieces from research-oriented living docs while keeping the living doc as the structured source surface.
what_proved: Living docs can support both machine-navigable structure and human-readable public synthesis when source commits and changelogs are controlled.
data.cost_to_apply: Requires care to keep the dossier from becoming the source of truth.

data.name: Used docs to hold mobile product seams
what_tried: Track mobile companion delivery, repo connection, feed transport, notifications, disconnect, and subscription refinement as living-doc surfaces.
what_proved: A living doc is useful when the risky part is not one file, but the connection between UX, state model, transport, and verification.
data.cost_to_apply: Needs frequent staleness checks because product seams drift quickly.

data.name: Made AI edits declinable structured patches
what_tried: Let AI propose card-level patches through a review/apply flow instead of mutating the substrate silently.
what_proved: AI can help fill or decompose cards without taking authority away from the document owner.
data.cost_to_apply: Requires schema validation, visible diffs, and clear decline paths.

data.name: Separated living docs from derivative readings
what_tried: Use sidecar readings for method, value, risk, and control interpretation while keeping the durable living doc about objective and current state.
what_proved: The distinction keeps docs useful for work instead of turning them into essays about the work.
data.cost_to_apply: Requires discipline in skills and generated artifacts.

data.name: Used a proof ladder for parser maturity
what_tried: Represent parser maturity as levels of proof rather than a flat list of implementation tasks.
what_proved: For parser work, the decisive surface is staged proof strength: one real email, next eligible email by predeclared rule, cross-merchant replay, shared outer-layer fixes, and higher autonomy claims are different kinds of evidence.
data.cost_to_apply: Requires the doc to keep proof claims, operating model, supporting runtime, and evidence boundaries separate.

data.name: Turned production failures into a governed hardening flywheel
what_tried: Treat production parser records as intake for deterministic local-first proof/evolution runs that end in completed evidence or a formal blocker.
what_proved: The method can govern real production-hardening work when it records intake shape, run-next authority, tooling, issue orbit, readiness gates, sanitized evidence, and blocker stops in one surface.
data.cost_to_apply: Requires strict process rules: no flexible item selection, no manual proof steering inside a qualifying run, and explicit issue creation for blockers.

data.name: Built bootstrap proof isolation around snapshot/reset/restore
what_tried: Make bare-parser bootstrap trials repeatable by snapshotting scoped graph state, resetting selected state, running the normal worker, restoring prior graph state, and comparing sanitized evidence.
what_proved: A living doc can describe not only the target proof, but the isolation discipline needed for the proof to mean something.
data.cost_to_apply: Requires careful distinction between isolated proof state, production graph state, and sanitized public evidence.

sections.title: Verification Checkpoints
sections.rationale: The ledger should convert reflection into concrete checks that can be run after future docs, syncs, or registry changes.

data.name: Check catalog drift against actual rendered docs
notes: Compare ~/.gtd/living-docs.json with existing source JSON and rendered HTML paths.
Flag entries whose source JSON is missing, stale, or not renderable.

data.name: Identify weak convergence-type boundaries
notes: Review docs where a section type is overloaded or where notes are carrying structure that should be typed.
Start with the most repeated types before adding new ones.

data.name: Promote repeated patterns into templates
notes: Look for stable section bundles that appear across monitors, issue runs, product workstreams, or reading-system docs.
Only promote a pattern when it has survived more than one domain.

data.name: Decide which docs need explicit sync skills
notes: Some docs can use the generic living-doc skill; monitors and specialized workflows may deserve dedicated sync skills.

data.name: Revisit this ledger after the next monitoring period
notes: Use the next round of monitor updates and implementation runs to test whether this ledger produces better method decisions.

data.name: Extract the TrackAndBack parser proof pattern
notes: Review the parser pipeline, proof ladder, production hardening, and bootstrap harness docs as one method cluster.
Decide which parts belong in the proof-canonicality template, which parts belong in a dedicated parser/proof skill, and which parts are TrackAndBack-specific process.
